exercises.title: Why 3D is the hardest modality
exercises.prompt: Explain the three things that make 3D generation harder than image/video: representation choice, data scarcity, and memory. Why does '2D-to-3D leverage' matter so much here?
exercises.hint: Many representations (mesh, voxel, point cloud, SDF, NeRF, Gaussians) each with tradeoffs; ~10M noisy 3D objects vs 14M+ clean images; voxel/NeRF memory explodes. 2D models are abundant → lift them to 3D.
exercises.solution:
**1. Representation.** Unlike images (a pixel grid) or video (a pixel volume), 3D has **no single canonical representation**. Options each trade off differently: **meshes** (compact, editable, but topology is hard to generate), **voxel grids** (regular but memory-explosive), **point clouds** (flexible but no surface/connectivity), **signed distance fields (SDFs)** (smooth surfaces, implicit), **NeRFs** (photorealistic novel views, but slow and per-scene), **3D Gaussians** (fast, high-quality, the 2023 breakthrough). Choosing the representation shapes the whole pipeline, and converting between them is lossy.

**2. Data scarcity.** There's a **massive data gap**: ImageNet alone has ~14M images and the web has billions, but the **largest clean 3D dataset (Objaverse-XL, 2023) is ~10M objects, most low quality**. 3D assets are expensive to author and capture, so you can't just scale a 3D generator with data the way you can for images.

**3. Memory.** 3D scales with a **cubed** dimension: a **512³ voxel grid is 128M voxels**; a useful scene **NeRF needs ~1M samples per ray**. Generation (sampling a full 3D field) is far more memory- and compute-hungry than **reconstruction** (fitting one known scene).

**Why 2D-to-3D leverage matters:** because 3D data is scarce but **2D image models are abundant and excellent**, the winning strategy is to **borrow 2D priors**: use powerful pretrained image/diffusion models to supervise or generate 3D (e.g. **score distillation** from a 2D diffusion model, or **multi-view diffusion** that generates many consistent 2D views and then reconstructs 3D). The lesson's point — '3D is the modality where 2D-to-3D leverage is strongest' — is that you sidestep the data wall by **projecting the knowledge of 2D models into 3D**, rather than training a 3D generator from scarce 3D data directly.

exercises.title: Gaussian Splatting and the multi-view-diffusion pipeline
exercises.prompt: Explain 3D Gaussian Splatting (what it represents and why it's fast), and the dominant 2024-2026 generative pipeline: multi-view diffusion → 3D reconstruction. Contrast this with the earlier per-prompt optimization approach (DreamFusion / SDS) and why the field moved on.
exercises.hint: 3DGS = scene as millions of colored 3D Gaussians, rasterized (not ray-marched) → real-time. Modern: generate consistent multi-view images with a diffusion model, then reconstruct 3D (fast feed-forward). DreamFusion/SDS = optimize a NeRF per prompt via 2D diffusion gradients — slow, Janus artifacts.
exercises.solution:
**3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS):** represents a scene as **millions of small 3D Gaussians**, each with a position, covariance (shape/orientation), color, and opacity. To render a view, the Gaussians are **projected ('splatted') and rasterized** onto the image plane — **not** ray-marched like a NeRF. Why it's fast: **rasterization is extremely GPU-friendly** (the same primitive as real-time graphics), so 3DGS achieves **real-time rendering and fast training**, with quality matching or beating NeRFs. It's an **explicit** representation (you can edit/move Gaussians) unlike a NeRF's implicit MLP. This 2023 breakthrough made high-quality, real-time radiance fields practical and became the backbone many generators target.

**The dominant 2024-2026 generative pipeline — multi-view diffusion → reconstruction:**
1. **Multi-view diffusion:** fine-tune a 2D image diffusion model to generate **several views of the same object from different camera angles that are 3D-consistent with each other** (conditioned on a text prompt or a single input image). This injects the abundant 2D prior while enforcing cross-view consistency.
2. **3D reconstruction:** feed those consistent views into a **fast (often feed-forward) reconstructor** (a Large Reconstruction Model / LRM, or a 3DGS/NeRF fitter) that lifts them into a **3D representation** (Gaussians, mesh, or NeRF) in **seconds**.
This 'generate consistent 2D views, then reconstruct' approach is fast, leverages 2D models, and produces usable assets from a **single prompt or photo**.

**The earlier approach — DreamFusion / Score Distillation Sampling (SDS):** optimize a **NeRF per prompt** by rendering it from random viewpoints and using a **frozen 2D diffusion model as a critic** — its denoising gradient (SDS loss) pushes each rendered view to look more like the prompt. No 3D data needed (pure 2D-to-3D leverage), which was the key insight.
**Why the field moved on:** SDS is **slow** (per-prompt optimization, often **tens of minutes to hours** per object), and suffers the **'Janus problem'** (multi-face artifacts — e.g. a creature with faces on both sides — because each view is supervised independently with no 3D consistency), plus **over-saturated colors and blurry geometry** from the high guidance needed. Multi-view diffusion + feed-forward reconstruction is **far faster (seconds vs hours)**, **more 3D-consistent** (views are generated jointly), and **amortized** (train once, infer fast) rather than re-optimizing for every prompt. So 2024-2026 shifted from **per-prompt 2D-distillation optimization** to **feed-forward multi-view-then-reconstruct**, often outputting **3D Gaussians** for real-time use.

exercises.title: The 3D consistency problem and where 3D generation is heading
exercises.prompt: 3D generation's core difficulty is producing globally consistent geometry from models that mostly understand 2D. Explain why multi-view consistency is hard, the quality dimensions that matter (geometry vs texture vs topology), how data scarcity is being addressed, and where the field is going (native 3D generation, scene-scale, integration with video/world models).
exercises.hint: 2D models don't inherently know 3D, so views can disagree → broken geometry. Good texture ≠ good mesh topology. Scarcity addressed via synthetic data, 2D priors, video. Future: native 3D latent diffusion, scenes not just objects, video models as implicit 3D.
exercises.solution:
**Why multi-view consistency is hard:** the leverage that makes 3D generation possible — using **2D image models** — is also its central weakness: a 2D diffusion model **doesn't inherently understand 3D structure**, so independently generated views can **disagree** (the back doesn't match the front, proportions drift, features appear/disappear), producing **broken or impossible geometry** (the Janus multi-face artifact is the classic symptom). Enforcing that *every* view is mutually consistent with a single underlying 3D object is the key challenge — addressed by **joint multi-view generation** (cross-view attention so views 'see' each other), **camera/pose conditioning**, **3D-aware architectures**, and reconstructing into an explicit 3D representation that *forces* consistency. But it remains imperfect, especially for unseen/occluded regions the 2D model must hallucinate consistently.

**Quality dimensions (they're separable, and you can be good at one and bad at another):**
- **Texture/appearance:** how good it *looks* from rendered views — often the easiest, since 2D priors are strong here.
- **Geometry:** the actual 3D shape — can be wrong (dents, floaters, melted surfaces) even when textures look fine.
- **Topology/mesh quality:** whether the output is a **clean, watertight, well-structured mesh** usable in a 3D pipeline (games, printing, CAD). Many methods produce great-looking Gaussians/NeRFs but **messy meshes** (bad topology, non-manifold geometry), so 'looks good in a viewer' ≠ 'usable asset'. Production cares enormously about this gap.

**Addressing data scarcity:**
- **2D priors** (the whole multi-view-diffusion strategy) — borrow from billions of images instead of millions of 3D objects.
- **Synthetic data:** render large datasets from existing 3D assets / procedural generation; use simulation.
- **Better/curated 3D datasets** (Objaverse-XL and successors), filtering for quality.
- **Video as 3D supervision:** video implicitly contains multi-view information (camera moves around objects), so **video models encode 3D structure** — a growing source of 3D priors.

**Where the field is heading:**
- **Native 3D generation:** instead of going through 2D views, train **3D latent diffusion** directly on 3D representations (latent fields, triplanes, Gaussian/mesh latents) — fewer consistency hacks, true 3D priors — as 3D data and 3D VAEs/codecs mature. This mirrors the image trajectory (pixel→latent diffusion).
- **Scene-scale, not just objects:** moving from single objects to **full scenes/environments** (much harder: layout, multiple objects, lighting, scale) for VR/AR, simulation, and robotics.
- **Convergence with video and world models (Lesson 10):** video models already learn implicit 3D and physics; the lines blur between **'generate a video flying around an object', 'reconstruct the 3D', and 'world models'**. A single system that understands space, time, and physics could generate consistent 3D, video, and interactive environments — the 3D, video, and world-model threads merging.
- **Real-time, editable, asset-grade output** (clean meshes + PBR materials) so generated 3D drops directly into production pipelines.
The throughline: 3D is gated by **data and consistency**, currently solved by **leaning on 2D/video priors**, and progressing toward **native 3D generative models and scene/world-scale systems** — the same compress-then-generate-in-latent arc as images and video, just the hardest and least mature instance of it in 2026.
